intro: Plain and Front land in similar territory on the hard numbers, so the decision comes down to fit with your workflow rather than a headline metric. Plain is built for apps and is not a fit for products without customer support. Front is built for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively and is not a fit for classic helpdesk workflows better served by zendesk or intercom. On the scoring side, Plain rates 4/5 for developer experience and 4/5 for transparency; Front rates 4/5 and 4/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Plain you accept that products without customer support, while Front comes with the reality that pricing escalates quickly with seats. Neither side is free. Plain's headline strength in the data is that in-app customer support chat. Front's headline strength is that unified email, sms, and social inbox. Read each side's best-for and not-for fields carefully before you pick; the shape of your team and workflow matters more than any single score.
quick_take: Plain is for apps; Front is for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively; decide based on workflow fit.
verdict_a: Choose Plain if you are apps.
verdict_b: Choose Front if you are ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively.

faq.a: Both list a seat pricing model in our data. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Plain to Front?
faq.a: Plain has medium lock-in (seat-based, data exportable via api); Front has medium lock-in (shared inbox, emails exportable). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Plain; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
Both Plain and Front score 4/5 on developer experience in our data, so DX isn't the tiebreaker here. Decide on fit, pricing, or lock-in instead.

faq.q: Is Front a good alternative to Plain?
faq.a: Front is built for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively and explicitly not for classic helpdesk workflows better served by zendesk or intercom, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Plain for apps, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
